title: Dog Legs Crossing Over When Walking: Scissoring Gait Causes & Urgency
meta_desc: Is your dog crossing their legs while walking? This scissoring gait often indicates a serious neurological issue. Learn when to seek emergency care.
content_html:
What is Scissoring Gait in Dogs?

When a dog's legs cross over each other while walking—often referred to as a scissoring gait—it is typically a sign of neurological dysfunction or ataxia. This occurs when the neurological pathways between the brain and the limbs are disrupted, causing the dog to lose track of where their feet are in relation to their body. It is often accompanied by stumbling, swaying, or knuckling of the paws.

Is It an Emergency?

Urgency Level: High. A sudden change in gait where the legs are crossing is considered a medical emergency. This behavior often points to serious conditions such as spinal cord compression, intervertebral disc disease (IVDD), or brain lesions. If your dog is also dragging their paws, tilting their head, or seems unable to support their weight, you should visit an emergency veterinarian immediately to prevent permanent paralysis or nerve damage.

Why Photos and Videos Help Triage

Capturing a short video of your dog attempting to walk is incredibly valuable for a triage team or veterinarian. Because neurological signs can be subtle or may fluctuate depending on the environment, a video provides an objective record of the incoordination. This allows the medical team to see the exact nature of the gait and the severity of the neurological deficit before the physical exam even begins, ensuring your pet gets the right priority in a busy clinic.

faqs.question: What are common causes of a dog crossing its legs while walking?
faqs.answer: The most common causes include Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD), spinal trauma, degenerative myelopathy, or tumors affecting the nervous system.

faqs.question: Can a dog recover from this type of walking problem?
faqs.answer: Recovery depends entirely on the underlying cause and how quickly the dog receives treatment. Many dogs recover with surgery or strict rest, but early intervention is critical for the best outcome.

faqs.question: What should I do while transporting my dog to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog as still as possible. Use a crate or a flat surface to carry them, and prevent them from jumping, running, or walking to avoid further aggravating a potential spinal injury.
